text: The XI robotic system is used to perform a left indirect inguinal hernia repair. The patient's full bladder is visible, and the indirect inguinal hernia is clearly identifiable.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision approximately at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), about seven to eight centimeters above the upper border of the internal ring, carefully incising the peritoneum and navigating between the layers of the transversalis fascia.
The surgeon proceeds medially, maintaining a pre-peritoneal approach during the XI robotic left indirect inguinal hernia repair, carefully navigating the anatomical layers.

The surgeon begins dissection in the lateral space, working within the preperitoneal area. Several tissue layers are visible laterally, which the surgeon plans to divide to reach deeper against the peritoneum.
The surgeon divides multiple layers of tissue, aiming to access the preperitoneal space. At least four or five distinct layers are visible as they are carefully separated.

The surgeon moves medially, staying in the preperitoneal space, revealing the transversalis fascia adjacent to the rectus muscle and the visible inferior epigastric vessels. Observing the thin peritoneum, the surgeon transitions to a more superficial layer, entering the retro-rectus space.

The surgeon is positioned laterally in the pre-peritoneal space, with a visible bridge of transversalis fascia separating the area. The surgeon prepares to cut through the transversalis fascia to connect the two spaces.
The surgeon navigates the surgical field, selecting the plane with the most areolar tissue, which is visible adjacent to the bladder on the right side of the screen.
The surgeon navigates the space of Retzius, bringing the lobular retrorectus fat down to expose Cooper's ligament, while the bladder remains positioned anteriorly.
The surgeon follows the areolar tissue to establish a dissection plane for inguinal hernia repair, minimizing bleeding by navigating through areas with visible areolar tissue.

The surgeon carefully navigates around Cooper's ligament, noting the presence of edema in the tissue, which is common in patients with a history of prostate radiation. The bladder is closely adhered to Cooper's ligament, requiring precise dissection.
The surgeon uses scissors to dissect tissue, employing a technique that involves gentle pushing and short bursts of energy for cutting.

The surgeon searches for the valley of areolar tissue between the fat associated with the femoral vessels and the fat connected to the bladder. Laterally, the surgeon attempts to position above the peritoneum.

The surgeon scrapes off tissue with squiggly blood vessels to facilitate the separation of the peritoneum from the testicular vessels and vas deferens. This technique aids in the takedown of the hernia sac, particularly in indirect inguinal hernias, minimizing bleeding during the procedure.

The surgeon divides the transversalis fascia anteriorly, with the vas deferens positioned at approximately the two o'clock position. Using short bursts of cautery, the surgeon cauterizes small blood vessels to prevent bleeding, maintaining a bloodless plane of dissection along the peritoneum.
The surgeon dissects anteriorly, then moves laterally, scraping blood vessels off the peritoneum using short bursts of energy to achieve hemostasis.

The surgeon carefully uses energy to cauterize small blood vessels during dissection, aiming for precision to minimize bleeding. Alternatively, the surgeon can scrape vessels off quickly, noting that most will stop bleeding even if torn, but prefers using energy for a cleaner dissection.

The surgeon pulls tissue towards the nine o'clock position and scrapes towards the three o'clock position, revealing the edge of the peritoneum. This visual cue helps identify the dissection plane, allowing for more aggressive use of cautery to expedite the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects laterally in the space of broad growth, avoiding the testicular vessels and vas deferens to prevent scrotal testicular pain.
The surgeon peels back the peritoneum to expose the vas deferens, ensuring that the mesh will have adequate overlap posteriorly.

The surgeon carefully dissects laterally, aiming to stay in a plane with minimal vascularity, leaving a shiny fatty layer on the pelvis to protect the lateral femoral cutaneous and genitofemoral nerves from injury.
The surgeon identifies the layer containing the testicular vessels, with a shiny layer left intact behind it. The femoral branch of the genitofemoral nerve is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon retracts laterally and medially to visualize the areolar plane between the bladder fat and the fat associated with the femoral canal. The dissection progresses towards the obturator space, ensuring the fatty plug within the obturator space remains intact.
The surgeon dissects down to the obturator space, ensuring the mesh can lay flat between the bladder and the ileum, indicating sufficient depth has been achieved.
The surgeon identifies the obturator vein, noting edema from pelvic radiation. The robotic, three-dimensional magnified view enhances precision in dissection, minimizing the risk of bladder injury.
The surgeon carefully teases away the bladder using an instrument, ensuring not to injure it. The pink tissue visible below the instrument is identified as the bladder.
The surgeon positions the mesh to tuck into the space of Retzius and transition into the space of Bagros, ensuring that no peritoneum or bladder tissue is caught behind the mesh.

The surgeon demonstrates the transition area of the mesh, pulling medially to reveal the areolar tissue that delineates the plane between the bladder and the fat adjacent to the iliac vein and femoral vessels.
The surgeon completes the dissection and prepares to switch from scissors to a mega suture cut needle driver, indicating readiness for the next phase of the procedure.

The surgeon identifies fatty tissue suspected to be a cord lipoma and attempts to separate it from the testicular vessels using a mega suture cut needle driver. The fatty tissue appears intimately involved with the testicular vessels, leading the surgeon to leave it undisturbed.
The surgeon displays a 16x12 cm ProGrip mesh, which is anatomically designed with a seam and consists of two pieces sewn together.

The surgeon ensures excellent lateral and medial coverage of the mesh, demonstrating how blunt dissection within the proper anatomical plane can easily create additional space without the need for scissors or excessive energy.

The surgeon places the anatomical pro grip mesh in a medial to lateral direction, ensuring the seam sits approximately two centimeters below the posterior edge of the internal ring. The mesh is folded for easier unfolding and is tucked between the superior pubic rami, pubis, and bladder. The surgeon checks that no peritoneum is caught between the lower edge of the mesh before finishing the unfolding process.
The mesh is positioned over the myopectineal orifice, crossing the midline with significant overlap in all directions, particularly laterally and posteriorly.

The surgeon begins closing the peritoneal flap using a 3-0, 180 V-lock suture, starting from the lateral side and moving medially. The edges of the peritoneum are closer together laterally, reducing the risk of ripping and tearing during the suturing process.
I always make an attempt to not drop the needle. And I always want to have the needle in the position for the next suture.
The surgeon performs three to four suture throws before pulling the suture taut, ensuring secure closure of the tissue.

Here I'm demonstrating to a case observer surgeon and explaining my technique, which is why you see some pauses. So here I'm showing him what not to do. That's the inefficient way of managing the needle. That's the efficient way of managing the needle.
The surgeon reaches the end of the suture line and performs two additional throws. The suture is pulled snug, tension is released, and the suture is cut flush against the peritoneum.
Thank you for watching.
